sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Christopher Watson, an 86-year-old male, presents in follow-up for adult hydrocephalus.
He has a history of 8-10 years of progressive gait impairment, cognitive impairment, and decreased bladder control.
He underwent shunt surgery on August 1, 2020, and has been followed since May 2020.
He reports that his walking has improved since surgery, but he still has times where he needs assistance.
His bladder control has leveled out, and he is wearing diapers, but he does not experience any discomfort or pain.
He reports improved cognition, feeling less lost when talking to someone, and his daughter notes that he is able to have a normal conversation with him again.
He denies headaches or pain where the shunt is, as well as pain in his belly.
He has not been hospitalized or sick since surgery.
Past medical history: Adult hydrocephalus, progressive gait impairment, cognitive impairment, and decreased bladder control.
Review of systems: Impaired ability to carry out daily functions, urinary incontinence, and wearing a diaper, continued walking impairment, and cognitive impairment.
Current medications: NOT MENTIONED.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/80, pulse rate 64, respirations 18, weight 174 pounds, and pain 0 out of 10.
Physical exam: The shunt site was clean, dry, and intact with a confirmed setting of 2.0.
The patient scored 26 out of 30 on the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) with spelling and 25 out of 30 with calculations.
He was able to get two of the three memory words with cueing and the third with multiple choice.
Gait testing using the Tinetti assessment tool revealed a gait score of 6-8 out of 12 and a balance score of 12 out of 16 for a total score of 18-20 out of 28.
Cranial nerves, pupils are equal, and EOMs are intact.
Face is symmetric, no dysarthria, and motor is normal for bulk and strength, coordination slow from no finger-to-nose.
Test Results: CT scan from 10/15/2020 shows a frontal hornstan at the level of firm and menroma of 4.6 cm with a third ventricular contour that is flat with a span of 10 mm.
There is a tiny amount of blood in the right frontal region with a tiny subdermal collection, and substantial small vessel ischemic change.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has made significant improvements in his gait impairment and cognitive function since shunt surgery, but still experiences urinary incontinence and wearing a diaper.
Problem list: Adult hydrocephalus; Gait impairment; Urinary incontinence; Cognitive impairment.
Plan:
I plan to leave the shunt set for another 2-3 months before re-evaluating the need for further intervention.
The patient will be followed up in 3 months.
Follow-up:
The patient will be seen again in 3 months.
